title: Sets Practice
instructions: Practice using sets for removing duplicates, set operations, and membership testing.
hints: Use set to track seen items, list for ordered result
Create full range set, subtract present numbers
Compare length of list with length of set

testCases.description: get_unique_elements should remove duplicates while maintaining order
testCases.validation: assert get_unique_elements([3, 1, 2, 1, 3, 4]) == [3, 1, 2, 4], 'get_unique_elements should maintain first-seen order'

testCases.description: find_common should return elements present in both lists
testCases.validation: assert sorted(find_common([1, 2, 3, 4], [3, 4, 5, 6])) == [3, 4], 'find_common should return shared elements [3, 4]'

testCases.description: find_missing_numbers should identify numbers absent from the range
find_missing_numbers should return empty list when all numbers present
testCases.validation: assert find_missing_numbers([1, 2, 3], 1, 3) == [], 'find_missing_numbers with all present should return []'

testCases.description: has_duplicate should return True when duplicates exist
testCases.validation: assert has_duplicate([1, 2, 3, 2]) == True, 'has_duplicate([1,2,3,2]) should return True'

testCases.description: has_duplicate should return False for a list of all unique elements
testCases.validation: assert has_duplicate([1, 2, 3, 4]) == False, 'has_duplicate([1,2,3,4]) should return False'

description: Practice using Python sets for efficient membership testing and set operations. Implement order-preserving deduplication with a seen-set, find common elements using set intersection (&), identify missing numbers using set difference (-), and detect duplicates by comparing list length to set length. Sets provide O(1) lookups that make these operations fast and elegant.
